title: Chapter 9 - TLS and HTTPS
description: Quick check on TLS and certificates.

questions.prompt: What is the main benefit of using HTTPS instead of HTTP?

options.label: Encrypted traffic that hides data from eavesdroppers

explanations.overview: HTTPS encrypts data and helps verify the server's identity.

byOptionId.b: Correct — HTTPS prevents passive eavesdropping by encrypting traffic.

questions.prompt: What does a TLS certificate prove to your browser?

options.label: That the page is always safe to use
That the server controls the domain name shown in the certificate
That the server has the fastest CPU
That the server stores no user data

explanations.overview: Certificates indicate which entity controls the domain and are signed by trusted authorities.

byOptionId.b: Correct — a certificate ties a public key to a domain or identity.

questions.prompt: Who signs TLS certificates so browsers can trust them?

options.label: Web browsers themselves always sign certificates

explanations.overview: Browsers trust certificates that are signed by trusted Certificate Authorities (CAs).

byOptionId.a: Correct — CAs sign certificates and browsers include a list of trusted CAs.
byOptionId.c: DNS resolvers map names to IPs, not sign certificates.
byOptionId.b: Browsers trust CA signatures; they don’t universally sign certificates themselves.
byOptionId.d: CDNs may have certificates, but trust comes from CAs.
